Quickly Clear Old Form Data or Stale Email History from Your Browser
Quick note on how to remove old data from a browser's "remember-what-you-typed" autocomplete on email or username fields.

Every time I type into an email or username field, the browser shows me a bunch of stuff friends typed in when I lent them the laptop. It doesn't break anything (I don't have their passwords), but it gets annoying:

This feature's called "form autocomplete" — it surfaces past form entries as you type (phone numbers, emails, etc.). It's a near-cousin of "autofill", which is heavier — autofill can be configured manually and fills a whole form on page load. So they're not quite the same thing.
Either way — when I have someone else's stale login history in there, I end up pressing the Down arrow a bunch of times to get past it. Annoying. Clearing my whole browsing history is overkill.
So here's how to delete specific entries.

Press the Down arrow to highlight the entry you want gone:
Cleaned up, only the entries I actually want:
Reloading the page won't bring it back.
I don't use Chrome day-to-day, but I sometimes test student projects in it and end up with a pile of junk autocomplete entries:

— but the Mac shortcut needs an extra
If the field has a saved password tied to that username/email on that site, deleting the autocomplete entry can also wipe the saved password. Worth knowing before you start cleaning.
Control whether Firefox automatically fills in forms

Browsers love to suggest every email and username you (or anyone using your computer) ever typed. This post covers the Shift+Delete trick to remove individual autocomplete entries in Firefox and Chrome without nuking your whole browsing history.
